hook: You already know that some numbers divide evenly into others and that primes are somehow 'special' — but you've probably just accepted these facts without asking *why* they must be true. Number theory is full of patterns that seem obvious yet are surprisingly deep to prove rigorously. In this unit, you'll discover how a handful of clean definitions and logical steps unlock some of the most elegant and famous proofs in all of mathematics.
concept: Every topic here uses the fundamental structure of the integers — how they divide, factor, and relate to one another — as the raw material for rigorous proof. The unifying idea is that arithmetic isn't just computation; it obeys precise logical laws captured by concepts like divisibility, primality, and congruence (the study of $\mathbb{Z}$ under $\mid$, primes, and $\equiv \pmod{n}$).

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write rigorous direct and indirect proofs about divisibility by translating '$a \mid b$' into the algebraic definition and manipulating it precisely.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Proofs with Primes and the Fundamental Theorem
topic_roadmap.description: Master how to use the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic — unique prime factorization — as a powerful tool to prove properties about integers and their factors.
Apply congruence relations to prove divisibility results and number properties, using the algebra of $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ to streamline arguments that would otherwise be tedious.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Famous Results: Infinitely Many Primes, Irrationality of √2
topic_roadmap.description: Reproduce and internalize two landmark proofs — Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many primes and the classic proof that $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational — understanding exactly where each logical step earns its power.
You will prove key properties of greatest common divisors by tracing the logic of the Euclidean Algorithm, including Bézout's Identity and its applications to linear Diophantine equations.
